statement: The EU acquis has expanded substantially since Iceland's negotiations were suspended in 2013. Major legislative packages adopted since then include: the European Green Deal and 'Fit for 55' climate package (2019–2024), including the European Climate Law, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), revised Emissions Trading System, and Renewable Energy Directive III; the digital regulation package comprising the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR, 2016), Digital Services Act (DSA, 2022), Digital Markets Act (DMA, 2022), and AI Act (2024); the Banking Union and Capital Markets Union reforms; the Common Agricultural Policy 2023–2027 reform; the Strategic Compass for EU defence (2022); and energy market reforms in response to the 2022 energy crisis. Any new accession negotiation would require Iceland to adopt all of these in addition to the pre-2013 acquis.
source_name: EUR-Lex legislative database; European Commission Work Programmes 2014–2025
caveats: Some of this legislation (particularly single market regulations) has already been or will be incorporated into the EEA Agreement and thus transposed by Iceland. The net 'additional' acquis that EU membership would require beyond the EEA is smaller than the total acquis growth. However, the non-EEA areas (CAP, CFSP, justice/home affairs, customs union) represent entirely new obligations.

related_entries.statement: The EU acquis communautaire is organised into 35 negotiating chapters for accession purposes, covering everything from free movement of goods (Chapter 1) to 'other issues' (Chapter 35). The chapters were expanded from 31 (used for the 2004/2007 enlargements) to 35 for Croatia's accession in 2013 to allow better balance between topics. Estimates of the acquis's size vary: the European Commission historically cited approximately 80,000–100,000 pages of legislative text, while a 2023 EUR-Lex analysis found that the EU had passed over 666,000 pages of law since 1957, of which roughly 26% (approximately 170,000 pages) remain in force. The acquis includes treaties, regulations, directives, decisions, recommendations, and CJEU case law. The volume continues to grow each year as new legislation is adopted.

The pace of EU legislative output has shifted markedly over the decades. In 1994–1998, the EU adopted an average of 2,907 regulations and 86 directives per year. By 2004–2008, this had fallen to 1,886 regulations and 112 directives per year. In the most recent period (2019–2023), annual output averaged 1,177 regulations and 48 directives. This declining trend in regulations reflects consolidation of the single market acquis, though recent years have seen major new legislative packages (Digital Services Act, AI Act, Green Deal regulations) that are individually more complex.

As of late 2025, approximately 13,000 EU legal acts (directives and regulations) had been incorporated into the EEA Agreement and transposed into Icelandic law since the Agreement entered into force in 1994. This represents the cumulative acquis adopted by Iceland through the EEA Joint Committee decision process.

citations.report_title: Engin fordæmi fyrir leið Íslands í átt að ESB

claims.claim_text: Margar breytingar hafa átt sér stað í alþjóðamálum undanfarin tíu ár sem hafa áhrif á samningaviðræður.
Efnahagslegar og hernaðarlegar breytingar í heiminum hafa breytt forsendum ESB-umræðunnar.

citations.report_title: Ræða þarf alla kaflana á nýjan leik

claims.claim_text: Hefjist aðildarviðræður á nýjan leik þarf að ræða alla 33 kaflana aftur þar sem löggjöf bæði ESB og Íslands hefur breyst frá 2013.

citations.report_title: „Við hefðum náð meiri árangri með því að vera innan Evrópusambandsins“

claims.claim_text: Losun alþjóðaflugs á gróðurhúsalofttegundum innan ESB fellur undir ETS-viðskiptakerfið.
Evrópusambandið er að dýpka innbyrðis samstarf sitt og verja hagsmuni sinna fyrirtækja vegna þróunar heimsmála.
